title: Kinetic Energy Calculator (KE = ½mv²)
shortDescription: Calculate kinetic energy from mass and velocity in joules.
description: A free kinetic energy calculator that finds KE = ½ × m × v² instantly. Enter mass in kilograms and velocity in meters per second to get the result in joules. Ideal for physics homework, lab work, and engineering checks.
metaDescription: Free kinetic energy calculator. Enter mass (kg) and velocity (m/s) to compute KE = ½mv² in joules instantly. Perfect for physics class, labs, and study.

result.label: Kinetic energy
result.empty: Enter a mass and velocity to see the kinetic energy.

sections.heading: What is kinetic energy?
paragraphs: Kinetic energy is the energy an object has because of its motion. It depends on the object's mass and on the square of its speed, so velocity has a far greater effect than mass.
The standard unit is the joule (J), where 1 J = 1 kg·m²/s². Doubling the speed multiplies the kinetic energy by four.

sections.heading: The kinetic energy formula
paragraphs: Kinetic energy is calculated with KE = ½ × m × v², where m is mass in kilograms and v is velocity in meters per second.
This calculator squares the velocity, multiplies by the mass, then halves the result, returning the answer in joules.

howTo.name: Enter the mass
howTo.text: Type the object's mass in kilograms (kg) into the mass field.

howTo.name: Enter the velocity
howTo.text: Type the object's speed in meters per second (m/s) into the velocity field.

howTo.name: Read the result
howTo.text: The kinetic energy in joules (J) is shown instantly using KE = ½mv².

faq.q: What units does this calculator use?
faq.a: Mass is entered in kilograms (kg) and velocity in meters per second (m/s); the result is in joules (J).

faq.q: What is the kinetic energy formula?
faq.a: Kinetic energy equals ½ × mass × velocity squared, written KE = ½mv².

faq.q: Why does velocity matter more than mass?
faq.a: Velocity is squared in the formula, so doubling the speed quadruples the kinetic energy, while doubling the mass only doubles it.

faq.q: Can I calculate KE for any moving object?
faq.a: Yes. The formula applies to any object with mass moving at non-relativistic speeds, from a ball to a car.

faq.q: How do I convert joules to other units?
faq.a: 1 joule equals about 0.239 calories or 0.000278 watt-hours; multiply the joule result by the appropriate factor.
